title: Does a Lie Become Truth When Repeated Again and Again?
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement. The Tamil reading copy restores the scan-confirmed Udangudi Stage III passage and `110வது விதி`, and repairs unambiguous OCR joins without changing any project figure. Entries 1 and 3 in the printed project list both identify Bhavani Kattalai Barrage II; that source duplication, as well as the repeated closing questions, is preserved. The canonical Tamil source file is unchanged.
paragraphs: Chief Minister Jayalalithaa and the Electricity Minister frequently boast that, during their tenure, they transformed Tamil Nadu into a power-surplus state. Indeed, paragraph 43 of the interim budget statement read in the Assembly on 16 February 2016 claims that “4,455.50 megawatts of generating capacity” were obtained by implementing new projects over the past five years. That is the language used in the budget statement itself. But did Tamil Nadu really receive 4,455.50 megawatts of generation from new projects implemented during those five years? If so, which power projects supplied how many megawatts? Under which government, and when, were those projects begun? Were they truly projects initiated during the AIADMK's past five years? Are Jayalalithaa or the minister concerned prepared to state the evidence openly?

As soon as the AIADMK came to office in 2011, paragraph 68 of the budget statement read in the Assembly on 4 August 2011 declared: “The state government will accelerate the North Chennai Stages III and IV generation projects, Udangudi, the Ennore link project and the Kundah pumped-storage project. These projects will provide an additional 5,100 megawatts over the next five years. During 2011–12, the following will be launched: the 800-megawatt Udangudi expansion project at a cost of Rs 4,800 crore; the 1,600-megawatt Uppur thermal power project at a cost of Rs 9,600 crore; a new 600-megawatt generating unit at a cost of Rs 3,600 crore to replace Ennore's forty-year-old thermal unit; and the 800-megawatt Tuticorin Stage IV generation project at a cost of Rs 4,800 crore.” Have all these projects been started?

Did Tamil Nadu really receive 4,455.50 megawatts of generation from new projects implemented during those five years? If so, which power projects supplied how many megawatts? Under which government, and when, were those projects begun? Were they truly projects initiated during the AIADMK's past five years? Are Jayalalithaa or the minister concerned prepared to state the evidence openly?

The AIADMK government's 2015–16 policy note placed before the Assembly itself supplies the answer. Under the heading “Present Status of Ongoing Power Generation Projects,” it described where these projects stood. The construction work for the Ennore thermal power expansion project, it said, was only then under way. The Ennore Special Economic Zone thermal power project was before the courts, and work had not begun. North Chennai Thermal Power Project Stage III was still at the tender-invitation stage. Work on the Uppur thermal project was also only at the tender stage. For the Ennore replacement thermal project, a work order for a land-based environmental-impact study was issued to a consultant on 16 July 2015. Even the study was ordered only six months ago. When will the project itself begin? Tenders were invited for Udangudi Thermal Power Project Stage I, but litigation over them remains pending. The government order for Udangudi Stage II was issued only on 3 February 2015; land is now said to be under selection. For Udangudi Stage III, work is said to be under way to invite tenders for offshore and onshore environmental-impact studies. Only now has a contract for tunnelling work on the Kundah pumped-storage project in the Nilgiris been finalised. The project still has not begun. The Sillahalla project, which Chief Minister Jayalalithaa later announced under Rule 110, has not begun either.

Every one of these details appears in the policy note placed before the Assembly by the AIADMK government itself. They make it definite and clear that not one of the power projects the AIADMK announced in 2011 has begun even after five years. Therefore, the claim read in last month's budget statement that “4,455.50 megawatts of generation” were obtained “by implementing new projects over the past five years” is wholly contrary to fact. The truth is that this entire 4,455.50 megawatts of generating capacity came from projects begun under the Dravida Munnetra Kazhagam government.

If Jayalalithaa asks whether not even one megawatt was generated under an AIADMK government, I will tell her: projects begun under her party's governments produced only 27 megawatts. But projects begun during the latest five-year AIADMK tenure, from 2011 to 2016, have not produced even a single unit of electricity. Of the 4,455 megawatts now cited in the budget, a mere 27 megawatts came from projects begun during the AIADMK's earlier 2001–06 tenure; the remaining 4,428 megawatts came from projects begun under the DMK government and from Central government projects. That is the true explanation.
I shall now set out in detail the projects from which action was taken to obtain electricity between 2011 and 2015, who governed when each project was begun, and the date on which it began.
1. **Bhavani Kattalai Barrage II:** Begun under the DMK government in 1996–97 at an estimated cost of Rs 99.15 crore. Capacity: 30 megawatts.
2. **Vallur, Unit I:** Begun jointly by NTPC and the Tamil Nadu Electricity Board under the DMK government in 2007, at an estimated cost of Rs 8,444 crore. Capacity: 500 megawatts.
3. **Bhavani Kattalai Barrage II:** Begun under the DMK government in 1996–97 at an estimated cost of Rs 99.15 crore. Capacity: 30 megawatts.
4. **Mettur Thermal Power Project, Stage III:** Begun under the DMK government in 2008 at an estimated cost of Rs 3,550 crore. Capacity: 600 megawatts.
5. **North Chennai Thermal Power Station, Stage II, Unit I:** Begun under the DMK government in 2008 at an estimated cost of Rs 3,095 crore. Capacity: 600 megawatts.
6. **North Chennai, Stage II, Unit II:** Begun under the DMK government in 2008 at an estimated cost of Rs 2,718 crore. Capacity: 600 megawatts.
7. **Vallur, Unit III:** Begun under the DMK government in 2007 at an estimated cost of Rs 8,444 crore. Capacity: 500 megawatts.
8. **Tuticorin Thermal Power Station, Unit I:** Begun under the DMK government in 2007 at an estimated cost of Rs 4,910 crore. Capacity: 500 megawatts.
9. **Periyar–Vaigai Hydroelectric Project II:** Begun under the AIADMK government in 2003–04 at an estimated cost of Rs 14.25 crore. Capacity: 2.5 megawatts.
10. **Periyar Hydroelectric Project, Unit I:** Begun under the AIADMK government in 2003–04 at an estimated cost of Rs 18.83 crore. Capacity: 4 megawatts.
11. **Periyar Hydroelectric Project, Unit II:** Begun under the AIADMK government in 2004–05 at an estimated cost of Rs 18.63 crore. Capacity: 4 megawatts.
12. **Periyar Hydroelectric Project, Unit IV:** Begun under the AIADMK government in 2004–05 at an estimated cost of Rs 18.63 crore. Capacity: 4 megawatts.
13. **Bhavani Kattalai Barrage III:** Begun under the AIADMK government in 2004–05 at an estimated cost of Rs 49.40 crore. Capacity: 10 megawatts.
14. **Periyar–Vaigai Hydroelectric Project III:** Begun in 2004–05 at an estimated cost of Rs 15 crore. Capacity: 2.5 megawatts.
15. **Simhadri Stage II, Unit I:** A Central government project. Capacity: 500 megawatts.
16. **Simhadri Stage II, Unit II:** A Central government project. Capacity: 500 megawatts.
17. **Kudankulam Unit I:** Begun by the Central government's Nuclear Power Corporation. Capacity: 1,000 megawatts.
18. **Neyveli Thermal Power Station Expansion II, Unit II:** A Central government project. Capacity: 250 megawatts.

Taking all eighteen projects cited by the Electricity Minister, projects for 3,860 megawatts of generation, estimated at Rs 22,915 crore, were begun under the previous DMK government; projects for a mere 27 megawatts, estimated at Rs 134.47 crore, were begun under the earlier AIADMK government; and projects for 2,250 megawatts were begun by the Central government. Tamil Nadu received 1,459.50 megawatts from the Central government projects.

The Chief Minister and Electricity Minister repeatedly speak to the people in a misleading way, as though their government itself planned 5,346.50 megawatts of power, allocated the money, invited tenders, completed the work and generated the electricity. Is there any truth in it? Concealing every one of these facts, they knowingly repeat the lie that “during the past four years, 5,346.5 megawatts of additional electricity have been obtained through new generation planned and initiated under the AIADMK government.” Whom is this drama intended to deceive?
Has even one megawatt been obtained from the numerous power projects announced after the AIADMK came to office in 2011? What became of them? Why has the minister not answered?

The Chief Minister and Electricity Minister knowingly repeat the lie that “during the past four years, 5,346.5 megawatts of additional electricity have been obtained through new generation planned and initiated under the AIADMK government.” Whom is this drama intended to deceive?
Has even one megawatt been obtained from the numerous power projects announced after the AIADMK came to office in 2011? What became of them? Why has the minister not answered?

Speaking in the Tamil Nadu Legislative Assembly, the then Chief Minister, O. Panneerselvam, made the broad assertion that, since the AIADMK government led by Jayalalithaa assumed office in 2011, 4,640 megawatts of electricity had been obtained through completed projects and medium- and long-term purchases. He supplied no underlying evidence or statistical details.

In the Governor's Address delivered on behalf of this government on 17 February 2015, page 25 stated: “The state's total power-generation capacity has increased by 3,358 megawatts over the past four years.” At the time, DMDK MLA Mohanraj said that he had sought generation details under the Right to Information Act and learned that not even one megawatt had been generated by a project completed after the AIADMK came to office. Newspapers too have written, with supporting evidence, that since returning to power the AIADMK has not conceived and completed a new generation project that has produced even a single megawatt.

Yet Jayalalithaa and the Electricity Minister refuse to heed any explanation. Like parrots taught a phrase, they keep repeating, “We have transformed Tamil Nadu into a power-surplus state!” Their vain hope is that a lie repeated again and again will become truth and mesmerise the people. But the people, who suffer power cuts every day, know very well that this claim they repeat without pause is like cotton hidden inside a blazing fire!
With affection,
M. K.
3 March 2016
